title: Knowledge Check: Cannabis
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 16: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this is a history and policy class, not an endorsement of use, and cannabis carries real risks, especially for developing teenage brains. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA national helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, who drew the line and who paid for it, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What changed between cannabis being accepted medicine and becoming a Schedule I drug?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"The politics, not the plant"},{"id":"q1b","text":"The plant became more dangerous"},{"id":"q1c","text":"The plant changed species"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Nothing changed at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Cannabis was accepted U.S. medicine from 1850 to 1942, then a Schedule I drug with, by law, no accepted medical use. The plant did not change; the politics did.","citation":"Lesson: The Green Revolution (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Where and roughly when was cannabis first cultivated?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Central Asia, around 10,000 years ago"},{"id":"q2b","text":"North America, in 1937"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Jamaica, in the 1900s"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It was never cultivated"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Cannabis was first cultivated in Central Asia around 10,000 years ago, one of the earliest crops in human history.","citation":"Lesson: Born in the Mountains"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Why did the Hindu Kush produce especially resinous cannabis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"High altitude, intense ultraviolet light, cold, and poor soil stressed the plant into making more resin"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Rich soil and constant water"},{"id":"q3c","text":"It was watered with chemicals"},{"id":"q3d","text":"The region was tropical"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Mountain stress, altitude, ultraviolet light, cold, and poor soil, drove the plant to produce more protective resin, the same stress-drives-quality rule as agave and wine.","citation":"Lesson: Born in the Mountains"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What legally separates hemp from marijuana?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"The THC level: below 0.3 percent it is hemp, above it is marijuana, though both are Cannabis sativa"},{"id":"q4b","text":"They are different species"},{"id":"q4c","text":"The color of the leaf"},{"id":"q4d","text":"The country of origin"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Hemp and marijuana are the same species, split legally by a single THC line of 0.3 percent. Same plant, different law.","citation":"Lesson: Hemp Comes Home"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Why is Indiana described as a prohibition island?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"It still prohibits cannabis while surrounded by legal Illinois, Michigan, and Ohio"},{"id":"q5b","text":"It is the only legal state"},{"id":"q5c","text":"It is an actual island"},{"id":"q5d","text":"It grows all the nation's cannabis"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Indiana is one of the most geographically isolated prohibition states, ringed by legal markets in Illinois (2020), Michigan (2018), and Ohio (2023).","citation":"Lesson: The Legal Patchwork"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What environmental trade-off comes with indoor cannabis growing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"A heavy carbon footprint from lighting, heating, and climate control"},{"id":"q6b","text":"It uses no energy at all"},{"id":"q6c","text":"It cleans the air"},{"id":"q6d","text":"It removes the need for water"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Growing cannabis indoors under artificial light demands large amounts of energy, giving it a significant carbon footprint compared with growing in the sun.","citation":"Lesson: Hemp Comes Home"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"How was cannabis used in the Hindu tradition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"As a sacred plant named in the Atharva Veda and tied to Shiva, with bhang still used at festivals"},{"id":"q7b","text":"It was banned in Hinduism"},{"id":"q7c","text":"It had no religious role"},{"id":"q7d","text":"It was used only as rope"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Cannabis appears in the Atharva Veda by about 1500 BCE, associated with Shiva, and the bhang preparation is still used sacramentally at festivals like Holi.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What was cannabis's status in U.S. medicine before criminalization?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"It was listed in the U.S. Pharmacopeia as accepted medicine from 1850 to 1942"},{"id":"q8b","text":"It was always illegal"},{"id":"q8c","text":"It was banned in 1700"},{"id":"q8d","text":"It was never used medically"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Cannabis was an accepted medicine in the United States, listed in the Pharmacopeia from 1850 to 1942, before it was criminalized.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Why did Harry Anslinger choose the word 'marihuana' over 'cannabis'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"To make the plant sound foreign and threatening, using racialized language to build support for prohibition"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Because it was more scientific"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Because doctors preferred it"},{"id":"q9d","text":"It was a random choice"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Anslinger deliberately used 'marihuana' to make the plant sound foreign and tied it to Black and Mexican communities in racialized testimony, building the case for prohibition.","citation":"Lesson: How Cannabis Became a Crime"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What did John Ehrlichman's account (reported by Baum, 2016) reveal about the War on Drugs?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"That the 1970 cannabis classification was connected to political targeting of Black communities and antiwar protesters, not pharmacology"},{"id":"q10b","text":"That it was based purely on medical science"},{"id":"q10c","text":"That cannabis is highly dangerous"},{"id":"q10d","text":"That it was an accident"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Former Nixon aide Ehrlichman's account directly connected the cannabis classification to political targeting of Black communities and antiwar protesters rather than to evidence.","citation":"Lesson: How Cannabis Became a Crime"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What did the 1937 Marihuana Tax Act do, and over whose objection?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"It effectively criminalized cannabis despite the American Medical Association's opposition"},{"id":"q11b","text":"It legalized cannabis nationally"},{"id":"q11c","text":"It was supported by all doctors"},{"id":"q11d","text":"It had no effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"The 1937 Marihuana Tax Act effectively criminalized cannabis, and it passed even though the AMA's counsel testified there was no credible scientific evidence of danger.","citation":"Lesson: How Cannabis Became a Crime"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What does the ACLU's 2020 data show about cannabis arrests?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Black Americans are about 3.73 times more likely to be arrested than white Americans, despite roughly equal usage"},{"id":"q12b","text":"Arrest rates are equal across races"},{"id":"q12c","text":"White Americans are arrested more often"},{"id":"q12d","text":"No one is arrested for cannabis"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The ACLU documented that Black Americans are about 3.73 times more likely to be arrested for cannabis, even though usage rates are roughly equal across racial groups.","citation":"Lesson: The Disparity"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What does the comparison of arrest rates and usage rates by race demonstrate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"The disparity comes from unequal enforcement, not from unequal use"},{"id":"q13b","text":"That Black Americans use cannabis far more"},{"id":"q13c","text":"That usage explains the gap"},{"id":"q13d","text":"That there is no disparity"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Because usage is roughly equal but Black Americans are arrested far more often, the gap reflects how the law was enforced, not how the substance was used.","citation":"Lesson: The Disparity"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"Roughly how large is the legal cannabis market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"About 69.8 billion dollars globally and about 47 billion in the United States"},{"id":"q14b","text":"About 1,000 dollars"},{"id":"q14c","text":"There is no legal market"},{"id":"q14d","text":"About 100 dollars"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The legal cannabis market is roughly 69.8 billion dollars worldwide and about 47 billion in the United States, supporting hundreds of thousands of jobs.","citation":"Lesson: The Billion-Dollar Leaf"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Why does an illicit cannabis market persist even where cannabis is legal?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Heavy taxes and regulation can make legal product more expensive than untaxed illegal product"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Legal cannabis is always cheaper"},{"id":"q15c","text":"There is no illicit market"},{"id":"q15d","text":"The government bans legal sales"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Because taxes and regulation raise the price of legal cannabis, untaxed illegal product can remain cheaper, so prohibition-era supply chains do not simply vanish.","citation":"Lesson: The Billion-Dollar Leaf"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What is the economic justice gap in cannabis legalization?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Prohibition's costs fell on communities of color, while legal profits flow mostly elsewhere, with under 2 percent of businesses Black-owned"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Everyone profits equally"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Only farmers profit"},{"id":"q16d","text":"There is no gap"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"The same communities that bore arrests and incarceration are largely shut out of the legal market, where Black ownership is under 2 percent in most states.","citation":"Lesson: The Justice Gap"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is a documented limit of cannabis social equity licensing programs?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"High upfront capital costs and a banking ban left equity applicants without money even with licenses"},{"id":"q17b","text":"They gave everyone free businesses"},{"id":"q17c","text":"They had no limits"},{"id":"q17d","text":"They banned all large companies"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Even with reduced fees, equity applicants faced high real-estate and inventory costs, and banks were largely barred from lending, so well-capitalized competitors captured markets first.","citation":"Lesson: The Justice Gap"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What is the border economics situation for Indiana?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Indiana collects zero cannabis tax while dispensaries cluster on its borders and collect tax on Indiana consumers"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Indiana collects all the region's cannabis tax"},{"id":"q18c","text":"There is no border effect"},{"id":"q18d","text":"Indiana sells cannabis to its neighbors"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Because Indiana prohibits cannabis, it collects no cannabis tax, while neighboring legal states collect tax on Indiana consumers who cross the border, a case of regulatory arbitrage.","citation":"Lesson: Indiana on the Border"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What is the economic argument FOR Indiana legalizing cannabis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Indiana forgoes tax revenue that instead funds schools and roads in neighboring states"},{"id":"q19b","text":"It would eliminate all taxes"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It would ban farming"},{"id":"q19d","text":"There is no economic argument"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Proponents argue Indiana loses tax revenue to Illinois, Michigan, and Ohio, where Indiana consumers' purchases fund public services across the border.","citation":"Lesson: Indiana on the Border"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"What is the economic argument AGAINST Indiana legalizing cannabis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Potential public-health, treatment, and workforce costs may offset or exceed the tax gains, with mixed evidence"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Cannabis cannot be taxed"},{"id":"q20c","text":"There are no possible costs"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It would raise too much money"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Opponents argue the public-health, treatment, and workforce costs could match or exceed tax revenue, and evidence from early-legalizing states is mixed.","citation":"Lesson: Indiana on the Border"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What propaganda technique is a 'fear appeal'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Using emotional fear rather than evidence to persuade, as in Reefer Madness"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Citing peer-reviewed research"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Presenting both sides fairly"},{"id":"q21d","text":"A measurement of THC"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"A fear appeal substitutes emotional fear for evidence, the central technique of the 1936 film Reefer Madness and Anslinger's campaign.","citation":"Lesson: Reefer Madness and the Language of Fear"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What is scapegoating, as used in cannabis propaganda?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Blaming a vulnerable group for social problems, as Anslinger blamed Black and Mexican communities"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Praising a group"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Citing real evidence"},{"id":"q22d","text":"A farming method"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Scapegoating attributes social problems to a specific vulnerable group, a documented feature of the racialized rhetoric used to criminalize cannabis.","citation":"Lesson: Reefer Madness and the Language of Fear"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What does the 'three sentences' lesson show about drug-policy language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"How you frame a situation prescribes a policy: punishment, treatment, or systemic change"},{"id":"q23b","text":"That all language means the same thing"},{"id":"q23c","text":"That words have no effect on policy"},{"id":"q23d","text":"That only one frame is correct"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Three sentences could describe the same person while implying punishment, treatment, or systemic change, so the framing does not just describe, it prescribes the response.","citation":"Lesson: Three Sentences, Three Policies"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is militarization in drug-policy language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Using war metaphors, like 'war on drugs,' that cast users and sellers as enemies"},{"id":"q24b","text":"A type of treatment"},{"id":"q24c","text":"A farming subsidy"},{"id":"q24d","text":"A neutral description"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Militarized language frames a public-health and social issue as a war, casting people who use or sell drugs as enemies to be defeated.","citation":"Lesson: Three Sentences, Three Policies"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What is reframing in drug-policy language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Changing the words to shift perception, like 'drug abuse' becoming 'substance use disorder'"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Banning all words"},{"id":"q25c","text":"A measurement of potency"},{"id":"q25d","text":"A type of arrest"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Reframing swaps one description for another to shift how an issue is perceived, such as moving from the moral 'drug abuse' to the medical 'substance use disorder.'","citation":"Lesson: Three Sentences, Three Policies"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is Schedule I, the category cannabis was placed in?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The most restrictive U.S. drug class, defined as having no accepted medical use and high abuse potential"},{"id":"q26b","text":"A category for safe medicines"},{"id":"q26c","text":"A tax bracket"},{"id":"q26d","text":"A farming permit"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"The 1970 Controlled Substances Act placed cannabis in Schedule I, the most restrictive category, defined by law as having no accepted medical use.","citation":"Lesson: How Cannabis Became a Crime"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"Which countries show different national legalization models?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Uruguay (2013), Canada (2018), and Germany (2024)"},{"id":"q27b","text":"No countries have legalized"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Only the United States"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Every country bans it"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Uruguay legalized nationally in 2013, Canada in 2018, and Germany partially in 2024, each with a different model of control.","citation":"Lesson: The Legal Patchwork"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"How does cannabis fit the season's sacred-origin pattern?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Like beer, wine, and tobacco, it began as a sacred and medical plant before being commercialized or criminalized"},{"id":"q28b","text":"It had no sacred history"},{"id":"q28c","text":"It was invented by a corporation"},{"id":"q28d","text":"It was always illegal"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Cannabis follows the season's pattern of sacred and medical origins, used in Hindu, Rastafari, and Chinese traditions long before it was criminalized.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What is regulatory arbitrage, as seen at the Indiana border?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Taking advantage of differences in rules between jurisdictions, like crossing a state line to buy legal cannabis"},{"id":"q29b","text":"A type of farming"},{"id":"q29c","text":"A medical treatment"},{"id":"q29d","text":"A propaganda technique"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Regulatory arbitrage means exploiting differences in rules between places, here Indiana consumers crossing into legal Illinois, Michigan, or Ohio.","citation":"Lesson: Indiana on the Border"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"Why did Reefer Madness persuade a 1930s audience yet look absurd now?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"It relied on fear and stereotype rather than evidence, and both the evidence and social attitudes have since changed"},{"id":"q30b","text":"It was based on rigorous science"},{"id":"q30c","text":"It is still considered accurate"},{"id":"q30d","text":"It never existed"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"The film used fear, false authority, and stereotype with no scientific basis, so as evidence accumulated and attitudes shifted, its claims came to look absurd.","citation":"Lesson: Reefer Madness and the Language of Fear"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What does this season lead with before discussing drug policy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Honest history and the real harms, with help resources like the SAMHSA helpline"},{"id":"q31b","text":"How to use the substance"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Where to buy it"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Nothing; it skips straight to policy"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Every Season 3 episode leads with honest history and harm, and points to help like the SAMHSA national helpline, 1-800-662-4357, never offering usage guidance.","citation":"Lesson: The Green Revolution (intro)"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What was Reefer Madness?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A 1936 propaganda film with no scientific basis, used to build fear of cannabis"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A medical study"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A legalization campaign"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A cannabis brand"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Reefer Madness was a 1936 propaganda film with no scientific basis, a case study in fear-based persuasion that helped justify prohibition.","citation":"Lesson: Reefer Madness and the Language of Fear"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Roughly how many full-time jobs does the U.S. legal cannabis sector support?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"About 440,000"},{"id":"q33b","text":"About 12"},{"id":"q33c","text":"None"},{"id":"q33d","text":"About 5 billion"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"The legal cannabis sector supports about 440,000 full-time-equivalent jobs in the United States, alongside more than 20 billion dollars in annual state tax revenue.","citation":"Lesson: The Billion-Dollar Leaf"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What is the broader lesson of cannabis social equity programs that fail to address capital access?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Addressing injustice without addressing capital and structural barriers can create the appearance of equity without its substance"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Equity programs always work perfectly"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Capital access does not matter"},{"id":"q34d","text":"There is no way to improve equity"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Without fixing the capital and banking barriers, equity programs can look fair on paper while well-funded competitors still capture the market, equity in appearance only.","citation":"Lesson: The Justice Gap"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"What does cannabis prove about the line between medicine and crime?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"It is drawn by politics and power, since the same plant was medicine, then a crime, then legal again"},{"id":"q35b","text":"It is drawn purely by chemistry"},{"id":"q35c","text":"It never moves"},{"id":"q35d","text":"It is decided by farmers"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Cannabis is the clearest case in the season of the line being drawn by politics rather than pharmacology, the same plant moving from medicine to crime to legal product.","citation":"Lesson: The Green Revolution (intro)"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What is the media-literacy skill this episode teaches for any drug-policy statement?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Asking who speaks in a given frame and what they gain from it"},{"id":"q36b","text":"Believing every official statement"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Ignoring all policy language"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Memorizing slogans"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Because framing prescribes policy, the key skill is asking who uses a frame and what interest it serves, whether the language is fear, medicine, or structural analysis.","citation":"Lesson: Three Sentences, Three Policies"}]}
